text: Amy Jones.

I agree with Miles and Jackie. A trades based one is best. You want somebody that's got experience with other builders in your case Andrew, so they understand because it just. They have their own nuances. You don't want to read somebody that specializes in retail doing your work as a builder.
Ms. Amy, what got in the way of your back costing me. My job? So simple.
No, honestly it's more just. I never saw it as a priority because.

Yeah we weren't. I didn't have any obvious leaks anywhere so while ever it's not hurting, it wasn't a priority. So yeah, I, I was doing really well at it and created a spreadsheet and was doing it regularly and I, I found that doing it regularly meant that I could like spot things before they actually became a problem.

And yeah, then figure out is it quoting, is it. You know, some things just go to shit. There's nothing to do about it. But yeah, again it's just fallen into the, the bottom of the pile for me with everything that's been happening with you know, end of year kids and stuff and not being in the office enough.
Totally. Would you share your system for back costing please? Just to give Karen an idea on where she could start.

Yeah, so mine's probably not as great as some of the others. I've literally just got a spreadsheet and I will sit and just add to it once a week because we do a lot of jobs too. I started out just doing like a couple of jobs a week. Not every job because I thought that the snapshot's better than nothing.

But yeah, because we've got everything in. In Servicemate the guys clock on and off jobs. Yeah, just jump into service, mate. Pull out the times that they run it. We don't have materials or anything so ours is pretty simple. It's really just labor really. And I've worked out our hourly running costs which. Yeah, I've just popped formulas into the spreadsheet.

So I just put in how many hours the guys were on the job, how many hours friends are allowed for the job and the spreadsheet figures out whether we were plus or minus. Yep, perfect. Let's do the same. Still using.
Happy to share too. I can send it through to you.

I don't. I think the problem with back costing is everybody thinks it needs to be complicated. It doesn't. It can be quite rudimentary. It's just to give you an indication of whether you're running down the right track or you're heading off track.

The most common Place that we lose variations that aren't charged for materials have been incorrectly assumed or fall into the variation category as well. Because there's something else that had to happen and we didn't issue a variation.
And then labor, given that it's a trap that all tradies make, we assume our team are going to be as efficient as us and they're just quite, quite simply not.

So we. It can take that can blow out quite quickly and can have a significant impact. So if you're tracking those three things, you tend to be on top of where you need to be. It doesn't need to be over complicated. I back cost every single job we do for our maintenance company with three team members at the moment. And it's not over the top, it's not hard to do. And that's done on the daily. So I do recommend that it's something that we're all doing.

If you want to start off with a couple of jobs a week or, you know, come up with a benchmark that you can work towards to give yourself an indication. I think one of the problems with back costing and one of the reasons it's often dropped is because it became.

It can become an emotional stout between the people in the business, particularly a couple, because it feels personal. When it goes wrong, you feel like you've done something wrong as the tradie typically. And so that conversation becomes a very personal one instead of it just being a factual one.
We just haven't allowed enough hours. That's not a. A personal statement, but it feels like it when you're the one doing the quoting.
So maybe coming up with a framework in which that can be presented can be very helpful for the longevity of working on back costing as well. Karen.

So with it, like you keep saying, like the quoting. So we do a lot of work, you know where somebody's hot water unit sort of gone and they've rang up and said, hey, we need that. So we don't actually sort of quote for those jobs. We just kind of do it and then send them the invoice. Can you back cost that or no, not really. You don't really need to because it's due and charge. You're charging for the hours that they're there and quoted jobs for the back cost. Correct.

All right, thank you. I just wanted just to make sure And Amy, that would be amazing if you didn't mind sharing your spreadsheet. Yes, that'd be great. Thank you. Awesome. Anything that's quoted, most definitely. I want you to back Cost. And I think when it comes to due in charge, the other problem that we can find with our team is they understand it's due in charge.

So they take as long as they take. Team need to be given time targets because they could go and do that exact same job the next day, the next week, the next month and take twice as long as they need to. But that time, it might have been quoted. So we do need to consistently give our team time targets.

Okay, you need to go and repair this hot water system or replace it. This is the time that we believe that it should take and then you can. That's part of performance management as well. So just to note on that so we don't let it go.

Yes, many of your job management systems will absolutely do this for you. Miles and Jackie, just sharing that with a score, it does it for them on every single job. Many of the systems that you use will have a functionality that can be used.
Great. All right, let's look at the questions for today. This gentleman isn't here. He's a newer.
Newer member. But I decided to include it anyway because I think that it leads me on to another conversation.
I'm just getting a sheet of paper to share with you. Okay.

So he is asking about the going rate for markups and how does he best reach the decision in the business. I'm not going to get into the very complicated markup versus margin conversation because it's.

I still haven't met a person yet that can effectively explain it in a small amount of time in a way that everybody that's listening can understand. It is almost impossible to explain how this works. I recently listened to one of my competitors podcasts on this topic. It was 45 minutes.
I got to the end of it and I had no idea what they just said. I could not understand what they were saying.

It blew me away. So I thought, well, I did take some reassurance out of that that I'm not the only one that can't explain it. But I. Even when working with Warwick, he would come to me and say, I need you to explain this because it needs to be in normal English. If I explain it, it gets too complicated.

So we did create a document. There is a document on the member site that will explain it to you. It makes it very, very clear so that you can understand in your own time, in your own space the difference between markup and margin.

Essentially, if we mark up something by 20%, which is generally the industry standard, that's not the margin. We're not actually taking 20% on top of that, it turns into a. I think it's 16.7%. And so the standard that we talk to so that you get your 20% margin is to mark up by, I say 35%. 33%. 33% will get you a 25% margin.
That's the power of markup margin. They are completely different, they don't make a lot of sense. It is a very, it's just maths, quite frankly. However, it's, it's a difficult concept to explain.

So when we're talking to new members, typically on all of your materials and your subcontractors, if you're a builder, I encourage you to mark them up by 33, 35% to give you that 25%. That's a really challenging conversation for lots of people. It feels a little bit overwhelming.
I typically hear, well, if I do that, then the client can go and buy it cheaper for themselves. Yeah, they absolutely can. But in this case, you're acting as a bank, you're creating a solution.

If you have the kind of clients that are focused on the markups or the margin, we're dealing with the wrong kind of clients and we haven't prepared them correctly. So this is part of a much, much bigger concept around being a solution based business instead of a business based on price.
I never want your clients to be choosing you on price. I want them to be choosing you on your service and your solution. And so this.

Concept of markup margin is very important for your business to ensure that you are doing it the right way to maximize your potential. So for the person that asked the question, sorry you couldn't be here today, but you need to be charging at least 33% as a markup on any of your materials or subcontractor labour that you pass on for you to get that 25% margin.
I'll ask the team to buy.
We're talking on cost, aren't we, Nick? Yes, we're talking on cost that we purchase the material for, correct? Yeah. Not the trade price, the cost price.

Correct? Absolutely. Because you're. I think there's a large underestimate of the amount of time, effort and the bank that you become for you to be able to have those materials in the right place at the right time. This is part of your service. None of us question it when we go to the supermarket.

None of us question it when we pay for fuel, or actually we might, we might get a bit shitty, but anyway, none of us question it when we go to the doctor or the specialist or the surgeon, I, I could not care less what those parts that they're putting inside me cost. Just providing they're going to extend my life.
And yet we seem to feel as though we are like a retail business where we're fighting against price for somebody else.

Like McDonald's and Hungry Jacks don't fight against price. Yeah, they've got items that draw you into their establishments that are marketed as cheap, but they're not competing on price, they're competing on taste. Woolworths and Coles compete on price. They're retail businesses. That's very different.

Again, Bunnings competes on price, but only on select items. There's a couple of items in that store, they call them loss leaders where they've bargained down with the manufacturer or the supplier of those items to have them super, super cheap. To give the feeling that everything in their store is cheap and it's not.
If you actually effectively went and price matched, you would find time and time again that they're actually quite expensive for products that aren't known value items.

So they have known value items that most of us shop around. For my pharmacy days it was Panamax. Everybody knew what a box of Panamax cost. Everybody knew what children's Panadol drops cost still do. Everyone knows what the price of nappies are. If you get that pricing right then you can charge what you want for everything else. That's retail. We're different.

There are sections of trades where it's important to have a similar price to others. For example, with Reese and Renee, air conditioning, cleaning or servicing for example would need to be pretty well in the ballpark of everybody else for them to be able to sell that service.

So there are definitely areas that you need to be similar. But for the most part I want you focused on solutions. Selling a solution, building out your sales process and your marketing plan to ensure that we're attracting the kind of clients that just want your solution.

Yes, yes, yes. Yeah, absolutely. Haven't used them. Look, if you can find a way that it might work for your system. Lauren, hopefully you're able to watch this back then. I think it could be a great idea. It's a great experiment. You tend to have really loyal people that work within in these systems because they're invested in getting a similar outcome to you.

I liken it to like business networking groups. They're very much the same. You know that you can be very culty, which I think is great. All that inter networking and recommending can be fantastic support for businesses.

So if you feel like this system can work for you, then I think it's very little lost by at least giving it a try. I don't think there would be huge reputational damage or huge financial loss by you giving it a Try definitely dip your toe in the water. If you can see a way that it will def. It would work for you.
Could work for you. Great question.

Karen. Barter card was a system where you literally had a barter card and you could exchange physical things or services or you would get discounts, for example, to try. And it's almost like a network marketing way to. To perform in that there's a loyalty to like.

I always shop at Dan Murphy's because I get a bulk buy discount whenever I go, you know, because I got my little Dan Murphy's card. Same kind of system. It was like one of the very first loyalty systems. They bought in and then they had.

They also had like warehouses that are a little bit like Costco, where you could go and buy bulk items at a less expensive price as well. That was all kind of part of the system. But I think these days.

Thank you, Miles. I think these days it's very much more about the loyalty of it. You know, if you go and shop at that particular place all of the time, you'll get a substantial discount, more than Joe Blow on the street. So it can definitely be worth looked at. But I also recommend thinking about your community. If you're. I don't know, think about real estate, for example, or you want to build or renovate. We all have skill sets and then we have mates that have skill sets.
There's a lot of exchange available there as well. Buttercup race. You can't be old enough about a car, but I reckon your dad would have been a good candidate for Buttercard.

Thank you for validating that. I'm old.

That'S the end of my questions for today. So I had some other questions for you guys. Does anybody have any questions before I move on? It's that time of year, everybody's tired. I have a question. Talking about bookkeepers.
What should they be taking on? Like, are we still responsible for our. Transactions and all that sort of thing. Or should they do everything?

Depends on what you want is the short answer. My bookkeeper does everything. Um, that said, bookkeeping's a lot easier now than it used to be because you've got things like Dext, or I just actually email all of my invoices to Xero and Xero then can automatically code it and then she goes in and approves it.

But for me, they do everything. I don't have time or capacity to do the book work for two businesses. It's not a lot for Tib, but there's a lot for QRM because there's so many invoices daily and so many jobs daily. Or don't do any of that. She does all of the data entry and she does the reconciling and the bas.
That's what she does for us.

I still take care of things like payroll. I prefer to keep control over that just so I make sure I'm ultimately responsible. So I want to know that it's done right. But previously in Tib, we had. Before I book workout, we had a different set of bookkeepers and they did payroll. These days it's all under my trust, so I prefer to do it myself just to keep an eye on it. And I also. We do all of the invoicing, but we have service mate, so it's very easy for us to invoice.

So we're still in control of that. And I would suggest for you guys, it's probably going to be easiest for you to keep in house as well, because you'll know when, you'll know what changes come. You'll know variations and things. I'd be doing all of that for yourself as well. Should they be paying bills or is that. Look, you can if you want to, but I don't advocate letting go of control of your bank account. I feel like that's.
You can set up rules, you can set up ways in which they can only access certain areas and pay things that are approved.

And I've seen time and time again it not work. Yep. If you've got a great one and you trust them and you're confident, by all means. If it takes a lot of responsibility off you. However, I want you to set up some kind of System so you can be checking on that on the daily. Yeah, there's far too many times I've heard that go wrong. We need to keep some level of control of that.
Miles and Jackie just sharing that there's does payroll transactions. Pardon me. P A Y G Bass and reconciling using dexterity.

Amy does the reconciling and payroll, but the bookkeeper does the reporting and lodging. Reese's bookkeeper does invoicing, reconciling, payroll. I approve timesheets. She pays bills. There you go. Reese has someone that uses his bank account. She's more of an admin support rather than a bookkeeper currently. Reese, I know you would be checking.

Can you explain your system for checking the bookkeeper? If they're accessing your bank account to pay bills and have you set up some structure so that there's only a certain area she can access or what does that look like for her?
Yeah, so she's got. Kind of the way we keep our bank accounts is I have two with one bank and four with another.

The like a profit first system. So the bulk of our cash actually sits in a complete other bank. And then we only have our operational in the accounts that she can access. So the two accounts she can access is the money that comes in and then the money that needs to be spent at the beginning of every month. She kind of allocates that move certain percentages to where they need to go. So she's only really got access to a small percentage of the total cash.

She's got a reasonably low limit that sometimes does. Sometimes doesn't work out for us. But basically that's. That's how I kind of restrict things. And also the way it's set up because we're with NAB primarily the way that it's set up there is she actually has a separate login for just our bank accounts.

So that basically it means that she can't just go ahead and transfer 40,000 bucks into her own private accounts. The only place she can do bulk transfers is between my own accounts. And then her payment limit is like five grand or something like that.
So it's quite. It's quite low, but it's enough to do base level bills.
The whole level limit is a pain in the butt, but it's a necessary evil.

It's. There's times that I've got to step in and occasionally if we do have to do larger transfers when we've got a bunch of supplier invoices all due on one day, especially this time of year, she'll kind of send me a message and say, hey, look, you know, this is going to take me seven days to do this transfer or I can just do it myself in one. So occasionally, if it's larger amounts, I'll step in and do it myself. But for the most part I have her handle as much as I can.
But it was very frightening letting go of that. It was very, very frightening letting go of that. But the headspace return that I got from not having to stress about it was. Was worth it in spades.
